content.text: Let me do my startup routine first, then dig into that session error.

# Commission Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per patron-sourced commission. Patron-subjective judgments only.
# Full artifacts live in experiments/data/commissions/<id>/ — see README there.
#
# IDs are writ IDs (w-*) from the Clerk. Pre-Clerk commissions used session
# IDs (ses-*) or ad-hoc labels — these are grandfathered with a note field.
#
# Fields:
#   id                — writ ID (canonical) or legacy session/label ID
#   title             — short description of the commissioned work
#   codex             — target codex name
#   complexity        — patron estimate (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21)
#   spec_quality_pre  — patron assessment of spec before dispatch
#   outcome           — patron assessment after review
#   revision_required — whether follow-up work was needed
#   spec_quality_post — patron assessment of spec after review
#   failure_mode      — if outcome != success, what went wrong (omit if n/a)
#   note              — meta info about the record itself (not content)
#
# Scales:
#   Complexity (Fibonacci): 1 2 3 5 8 13 21
#   Spec quality: strong | adequate | weak
#   Outcome: success | partial | wrong | abandoned
#   Failure mode: spec_ambiguous | requirement_wrong | execution_error | complexity_overrun | broken | incomplete

commissions:
  - id: ses-93ad1c4c
    title: "Implement Clerk apparatus (MVP)"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 5
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: abandoned
    failure_mode: execution_error
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: C002
    title: "Implement Dispatch apparatus"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 3
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is ad-hoc label, not writ ID. Never dispatched — superseded by ses-0334962d."

  - id: ses-19194146
    title: "Implement Clerk apparatus (MVP) — re-dispatch"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 5
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: partial
    revision_required: true
    spec_quality_post: adequate
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: ses-0334962d
    title: "Implement Dispatch apparatus"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 3
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: strong
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: ses-2149b518
    title: "Scriptorium seal/push sync hardening"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: strong
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: ses-053770d1
    title: "Clerk apparatus — align implementation with spec"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: partial
    revision_required: true
    spec_quality_post: strong
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: ses-8cdbbc39
    title: "Dispatch apparatus — align implementation with spec"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 1
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: strong
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: w-mnhiv9lbbccc525bf153
    title: "Normalize ID Formats Across Apparatus"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: abandoned
    spec_quality_post: strong
    failure_mode: execution_error
    reviewed_by_ethnographer: true
    note: "Anima described changes but didn't commit. No commit instruction in prompt."

  - id: w-mnhjg4deb43b581c763e
    title: "Normalize ID Formats Across Apparatus"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: adequate
    outcome: partial
    revision_required: true
    spec_quality_post: adequate
    failure_mode: spec_ambiguous
    reviewed_by_ethnographer: true
    note: "Re-dispatch of w-mnhiv9lbbccc525bf153. Core extraction solid but broke animator test and missed Clerk migration."

  - id: w-mnhl7kt97066dce908b2
    title: "Normalize ID Formats — Fixup"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: strong
    note: "First commission using exhaustive spec style. Clean first-try success — supports X013 finding."
    meta: "Left latent build failures (Zod 4 compat, animator test regex, publish glob) not captured in quality review — outside diff range. Fixed by next anima (w-mnho6jxd) as preamble. Shared git identity prevented attribution."

  - id: w-mnho6jxd-c8139f50006c
    title: "Anima Git Identity"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 5
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: true
    spec_quality_post: adequate
    meta: "Quality score contaminated by shared git identity — the very problem this commission fixes. 3 of 5 commits in scored range are cleanup from prior commissions (Zod compat, test regex, publish glob). Composite artificially depressed."

  - id: w-mnhpydhp-31255960bc0c
    title: "test-singleton-fix"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 0
    outcome: abandoned
    note: "Coco test writ — posted to verify commission-post worked after fixing Laboratory singleton bug (pnpm file: vs link: resolution). Anima picked it up on next dispatch instead of the intended commission. Not real work."

  - id: w-mnhq1swd-b4f2e7683639
    title: "Anima Git Identity — Test Coverage"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    outcome: cancelled
    note: "Cancelled — dispatch picked up stale test writ (w-mnhpydhp) instead of this one. Re-dispatched as a fresh commission."

  - id: w-mnhq6gpv-a979fbca3213
    title: "Anima Git Identity — Test Coverage"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: strong

  - id: w-mnhq8v8z-0b0f4f13e815
    title: "Plugin Install — Use `link:` Protocol for Local Directories"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: adequate

  - id: w-mnhr98jj-9a4fd05dd0a8
    title: "Rigging Review Loop — Design Spec"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 5
    spec_quality_pre: adequate
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: adequate
    note: "First spec-writing commission. Seed spec was deliberately thin — anima expanded into 548-line design doc. Scorer dinged test_quality (1) because it's a doc, not code."

  - id: w-mnhsn4xw-39672cfe2dc8
    title: "Fix Committer Identity Override for Signature Verification"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 1
    spec_quality_pre: adequate
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: adequate
    note: "One-line commission, no formal spec. Title in Clerk is the full body text (dispatch.sh extracts first line as title). This commit itself is unverified on GitHub — chicken-and-egg, the fix only applies to subsequent dispatches."

  - id: w-mnhy86ga-fedf0135a60c
    title: "The Fabricator — API Contract"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 5
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: true
    spec_quality_post: adequate
    note: "Correct implementation, all requirements met, typechecks clean. No tests — spec didn't require them. Eager singleton in index.ts worth reviewing. First commission scored via author-email commit discovery."

  - id: w-mni0ugjx-05b64cee0466

content.content:
1	# Session Summary
2	
3	## What we did
4	
5	- **Diagnosed the absolute-path pipeline bug** — plan agents (reader/analyst/writer) running from the sanctum wrote `/workspace/nexus/...` paths into specs. Animas followed those paths out of their worktrees, committing to the wrong repo.
6	- **Implemented temp-clone pipeline execution** — `plan-review.ts` now shallow-clones the target codex to a temp dir per pipeline step, sets `cwd` there, references agent files by absolute path, cleans up on exit. Added `cloneCodex()`, `getCodexRemoteUrl()` helpers, `os` import.
7	- **Captured codex at brief creation** — added `codex` field to `.meta.yml`, required in `POST /api/specs`, codex dropdown added to new-spec form in workshop UI.
8	- **Removed codex from dispatch form** — dispatch now reads codex from `.meta.yml` exclusively.
9	- **Updated all three plan agent instructions** — removed hardcoded `/workspace/nexus/` paths and Project Context section, added Working Environment section, updated all output paths to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/`.
10	- **Backfilled existing specs** — all 7 `.meta.yml` files now have `codex: nexus`.
11	- **Updated nexus CLAUDE.md** — project structure now reflects all current plugins (added clerk, codexes, fabricator, spider, parlour, walker; removed stale guild-starter-kit and packages-deprecated).
12	
13	## Decisions made and/or deferred
14	
15	- **Temp clone over path rewriting** — Sean pushed for running agents inside the codex rather than rewriting paths at dispatch time. Cleaner, generalizable.
16	- **CLAUDE.md auto-discovery for plan agents** — plan agents don't use `--setting-sources user`, so the codex's CLAUDE.md is picked up naturally. No hardcoded doc references needed in agent instructions.
17	- **No orientation section in nexus CLAUDE.md** — Sean pointed out this would info-dump every agent. Anima curriculum is the right layer for prescribing reading.
18	- **Old specs left as-is** — all already dispatched/completed; not worth regenerating.
19	
20	## Next steps & open questions
21	
22	- Test the full pipeline end-to-end: create a new spec in the workshop and verify relative paths throughout
23	- `--setting-sources user` on guild-side animas — Sean noted he's not sure why it was added, may relax it for per-codex orientation. Future work.
24	
25	### Notable moments
26	
27	- **"probably need to run them from just inside the target codex with no outside pointers"** — Sean redirecting from path-rewriting approaches to the cleaner architectural fix. The insight: if the agent's cwd IS the codex, relative paths are the natural default.
28	- **"don't give explicit instructions for using relative paths — that would be the default, if we weren't 'busting out' of the repo"** — recognizing the root cause is environmental, not instructional.
29	- **"part of the whole 'anima curriculum' thing is being able to not info-dump every agent"** — Sean catching that a blanket CLAUDE.md orientation section defeats the purpose of the curriculum system.
30	
31	### Experiment observations
32	
33	- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco implemented all changes (~100 lines net in plan-review.ts, 3 agent files updated, 7 meta files backfilled, nexus CLAUDE.md updated). Sean directed architecture throughout. No commission considered — this is pipeline infrastructure.
34

file.content:
# Session Summary

## What we did

- **Diagnosed the absolute-path pipeline bug** — plan agents (reader/analyst/writer) running from the sanctum wrote `/workspace/nexus/...` paths into specs. Animas followed those paths out of their worktrees, committing to the wrong repo.
- **Implemented temp-clone pipeline execution** — `plan-review.ts` now shallow-clones the target codex to a temp dir per pipeline step, sets `cwd` there, references agent files by absolute path, cleans up on exit. Added `cloneCodex()`, `getCodexRemoteUrl()` helpers, `os` import.
- **Captured codex at brief creation** — added `codex` field to `.meta.yml`, required in `POST /api/specs`, codex dropdown added to new-spec form in workshop UI.
- **Removed codex from dispatch form** — dispatch now reads codex from `.meta.yml` exclusively.
- **Updated all three plan agent instructions** — removed hardcoded `/workspace/nexus/` paths and Project Context section, added Working Environment section, updated all output paths to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/`.
- **Backfilled existing specs** — all 7 `.meta.yml` files now have `codex: nexus`.
- **Updated nexus CLAUDE.md** — project structure now reflects all current plugins (added clerk, codexes, fabricator, spider, parlour, walker; removed stale guild-starter-kit and packages-deprecated).

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Temp clone over path rewriting** — Sean pushed for running agents inside the codex rather than rewriting paths at dispatch time. Cleaner, generalizable.
- **CLAUDE.md auto-discovery for plan agents** — plan agents don't use `--setting-sources user`, so the codex's CLAUDE.md is picked up naturally. No hardcoded doc references needed in agent instructions.
- **No orientation section in nexus CLAUDE.md** — Sean pointed out this would info-dump every agent. Anima curriculum is the right layer for prescribing reading.
- **Old specs left as-is** — all already dispatched/completed; not worth regenerating.

## Next steps & open questions

- Test the full pipeline end-to-end: create a new spec in the workshop and verify relative paths throughout
- `--setting-sources user` on guild-side animas — Sean noted he's not sure why it was added, may relax it for per-codex orientation. Future work.

### Notable moments

- **"probably need to run them from just inside the target codex with no outside pointers"** — Sean redirecting from path-rewriting approaches to the cleaner architectural fix. The insight: if the agent's cwd IS the codex, relative paths are the natural default.
- **"don't give explicit instructions for using relative paths — that would be the default, if we weren't 'busting out' of the repo"** — recognizing the root cause is environmental, not instructional.
- **"part of the whole 'anima curriculum' thing is being able to not info-dump every agent"** — Sean catching that a blanket CLAUDE.md orientation section defeats the purpose of the curriculum system.

### Experiment observations

- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco implemented all changes (~100 lines net in plan-review.ts, 3 agent files updated, 7 meta files backfilled, nexus CLAUDE.md updated). Sean directed architecture throughout. No commission considered — this is pipeline infrastructure.

content.content:
1	# Session Summary
2	
3	## What we did
4	
5	- **Diagnosed `claude --print` bug** — writ bodies starting with `---` (YAML frontmatter) were rejected by the claude CLI's Commander parser as unknown options. Fixed in claude-code provider by piping prompt via stdin (`--print -`) instead of passing as CLI arg. Committed as `2615bde` in `/workspace/nexus`.
6	- **Diagnosed why quality scores weren't running** — traced from Laboratory CDC → quality trigger → instrument review → `resolve-commits.sh` extractor failing because the bare clone was stale. Root cause: specs contained absolute paths (`/workspace/nexus/...`), so animas committed directly to `/workspace/nexus` instead of their worktrees. Bare clone never got the commits, seal saw nothing to merge (`inscriptionsSealed: 0`).
7	- **Cleaned up bare clone** — fetched 13 missing commits from origin, deleted 3 orphaned worktrees and draft branches.
8	- **Backfilled quality scores** — ran all 3 instruments on 6 completed commissions missing scores. Committed as `6db9e13`.
9	- **Updated commission log** — filled in spec_quality_pre, outcome, and notes for 7 commissions. Documented the cross-contamination between `w-mnjxdf9z` (seal) and `w-mnjxdvcq` (dispatch removal) as a symptom of the shared working directory problem.
10	
11	## Decisions made and/or deferred
12	
13	- **Seal should include push** — Sean agreed seal() should mean "finalized and pushed." Implemented by w-mnjxdvcq (via cross-contamination). No separate push step in the spider pipeline.
14	- **Stdin for claude prompts** — `--print -` is the standard approach going forward. Avoids both dash-prefix parsing and arg length limits.
15	- **Patron reviews deferred** — 11 older commissions still have blank review.md templates.
16	
17	## Next steps & open questions
18	
19	- Verify next commission uses worktrees correctly (relative paths fix is in place)
20	- 11 blank patron reviews from earlier commissions
21	- The seal commission (`w-mnjxdf9z`) technically needs a re-dispatch for the test-only gap, but the work is done via cross-contamination — probably not worth re-dispatching
22	
23	### Notable moments
24	
25	- **The full debugging chain** — `--print` bug → bare clone stale → worktree bypass → cross-contamination. Four seemingly separate problems traced to one root cause: absolute paths in specs. Sean had already fixed the planning agents before we finished the diagnosis.
26	- **"should pushing just be part of sealing? what is 'sealing' now?"** — Sean questioning whether the abstraction boundary between seal and push still made sense given the Spider handles everything end-to-end. Led to collapsing them.
27	- **"Do we know if the cross-contaminated work was actually the result of our 'shared work in the /workspace/nexus' problem?"** — Sean correctly identified that the apparent scope leak was a symptom, not a novel failure mode. Important for experiment accuracy.
28	
29	### Experiment observations
30	
31	- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco fixed the `--print` stdin bug (one file, ~20 lines), ran quality scoring on 6 commissions, updated commission log, cleaned bare clone. All directed by Sean. No commission considered — operational maintenance and data backfill.
32	- **X013 (Commission Outcomes):** New failure mode documented: concurrent commissions sharing a working directory leads to cross-contamination of work scope. Not a spec quality issue — isolation infrastructure failure. Two commissions both scored "partial" despite combined output being correct.
33

file.content:
# Session Summary

## What we did

- **Diagnosed `claude --print` bug** — writ bodies starting with `---` (YAML frontmatter) were rejected by the claude CLI's Commander parser as unknown options. Fixed in claude-code provider by piping prompt via stdin (`--print -`) instead of passing as CLI arg. Committed as `2615bde` in `/workspace/nexus`.
- **Diagnosed why quality scores weren't running** — traced from Laboratory CDC → quality trigger → instrument review → `resolve-commits.sh` extractor failing because the bare clone was stale. Root cause: specs contained absolute paths (`/workspace/nexus/...`), so animas committed directly to `/workspace/nexus` instead of their worktrees. Bare clone never got the commits, seal saw nothing to merge (`inscriptionsSealed: 0`).
- **Cleaned up bare clone** — fetched 13 missing commits from origin, deleted 3 orphaned worktrees and draft branches.
- **Backfilled quality scores** — ran all 3 instruments on 6 completed commissions missing scores. Committed as `6db9e13`.
- **Updated commission log** — filled in spec_quality_pre, outcome, and notes for 7 commissions. Documented the cross-contamination between `w-mnjxdf9z` (seal) and `w-mnjxdvcq` (dispatch removal) as a symptom of the shared working directory problem.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Seal should include push** — Sean agreed seal() should mean "finalized and pushed." Implemented by w-mnjxdvcq (via cross-contamination). No separate push step in the spider pipeline.
- **Stdin for claude prompts** — `--print -` is the standard approach going forward. Avoids both dash-prefix parsing and arg length limits.
- **Patron reviews deferred** — 11 older commissions still have blank review.md templates.

## Next steps & open questions

- Verify next commission uses worktrees correctly (relative paths fix is in place)
- 11 blank patron reviews from earlier commissions
- The seal commission (`w-mnjxdf9z`) technically needs a re-dispatch for the test-only gap, but the work is done via cross-contamination — probably not worth re-dispatching

### Notable moments

- **The full debugging chain** — `--print` bug → bare clone stale → worktree bypass → cross-contamination. Four seemingly separate problems traced to one root cause: absolute paths in specs. Sean had already fixed the planning agents before we finished the diagnosis.
- **"should pushing just be part of sealing? what is 'sealing' now?"** — Sean questioning whether the abstraction boundary between seal and push still made sense given the Spider handles everything end-to-end. Led to collapsing them.
- **"Do we know if the cross-contaminated work was actually the result of our 'shared work in the /workspace/nexus' problem?"** — Sean correctly identified that the apparent scope leak was a symptom, not a novel failure mode. Important for experiment accuracy.

### Experiment observations

- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco fixed the `--print` stdin bug (one file, ~20 lines), ran quality scoring on 6 commissions, updated commission log, cleaned bare clone. All directed by Sean. No commission considered — operational maintenance and data backfill.
- **X013 (Commission Outcomes):** New failure mode documented: concurrent commissions sharing a working directory leads to cross-contamination of work scope. Not a spec quality issue — isolation infrastructure failure. Two commissions both scored "partial" despite combined output being correct.

# Session Summary

## What we did

- **Fixed codex dropdown styling in brief form** — the `<select>` in `.new-spec` was missing from the CSS rules that styled `.new-spec input`. Added `select` to both the base and `:focus` rules in `bin/plan-review.ts`.
- **Added writ linking fields to the brief form** — two combobox fields (link type + target writ) using native `<input>` + `<datalist>` pattern:
  - Link type: freetext with "fixes" and "depends on" as suggestions
  - Writ ID: freetext with last 15 writs as suggestions (showing id + title), with live validation (✓/✗/⋯)
  - Both fields optional; if one is filled, both required
- **New API endpoints** in plan-review.ts: `GET /api/writs` (last 15 writs for dropdown), `GET /api/writs/:id/exists` (freetext validation)
- **Link storage and execution**: link saved in `.meta.yml`, `clerk.link()` called after successful dispatch
- **Fixed datalist duplication**: writ ID was showing twice in dropdown rows (as `value` and in label text). Changed to show ID as value, title as label.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- Used native `<input list>` + `<datalist>` for combobox behavior rather than a custom dropdown component. Simpler, inherits existing styling, gives both freetext and select-from-list.
- Link is stored in spec meta and executed post-dispatch (not at spec creation time) since we need the new writ ID as the link source.
- Only one link supported for now — Sean's explicit scope choice.

## Next steps & open questions

- Test the link UI end-to-end with the running workshop (no broken commissions available to test "fixes" link naturally — use a throwaway spec or wait for something to break)
- Consider whether link type suggestions should expand beyond "fixes" and "depends on"

### Experiment observations

- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco did implementation work on plan-review.ts — Sean directed the feature (writ linking in brief form), Coco implemented. ~60 lines of CSS/HTML/JS added. This is the plan workshop tool which lives in the sanctum, not commissionable to the guild.

file.content:
# Session Summary

## What we did

- **Plan workshop: git auto-commit+push** — Added `autoCommitSpec()` helper to `bin/plan-review.ts` that non-blockingly commits and pushes spec files at three lifecycle boundaries: brief creation, writer completion, and dispatch. (`b4f6389`)
- **Committed orphaned spec files** — 8 spec directories were sitting untracked/staged across `specs/`. Bundled them into the same commit to clean the slate.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Option 2 (lifecycle commits) over granular or every-write commits** — Coco presented three options for when to auto-commit. Sean chose Option 2: commit at spec creation, writer completion, and dispatch. Rationale: meaningful commits without noise; intermediate pipeline artifacts (observations, scope) are transient.
- **Push on every commit** — No deferred/batched push. Each autocommit also pushes immediately.
- **Deferred: concurrent push conflicts** — If two specs finish their writer step simultaneously, the second push could fail on non-fast-forward. Acknowledged as unlikely given pipeline duration; will address if it surfaces.

## Next steps & open questions

- Test the auto-commit flow end-to-end by creating a new spec in the workshop
- The modified `.meta.yml` files for `plugin-dependency-crash-blocks-all` and `scriptorium-doc-has-multiple-stale` appeared dirty after commit — may be touched by the running workshop. Worth checking.

### Notable moments

- **"lots of spec files sitting around.. can the workshop autocommit+push files it creates/changes?"** — Sean identifying operational friction (manual git hygiene for workshop output) and asking for automation. Short session, clear ask, clean implementation.

### Experiment observations

- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco implemented the autocommit feature (~19 lines of new code + 3 hook-site insertions). Sean chose the approach from options Coco presented. No commission considered — this is sanctum tooling (plan-review.ts lives in nexus-mk2).

.claude/skills/plan-feature/SKILL.md

1	---
2	name: plan-analyst
3	description: Scope and decision analyst — decomposes a brief into reviewable scope items and structured design decisions
4	model: opus
5	tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Write
6	---
7	
8	<!-- Version: 2026-04-04. Update this when instructions change materially. -->
9	
10	# Plan Analyst — Scope & Decision Analyst
11	
12	## Role
13	
14	You are **Plan Analyst**, an autonomous analysis agent. You take a brief and produce two things: a **scope breakdown** of what the feature entails, and a **structured set of design decisions** with recommended defaults. These outputs go to the patron for review before a spec is written.
15	
16	You sit on the patron's side of the boundary. You are not a guild member.
17	
18	## Working Environment
19	
20	You run from inside a clone of the target codex. All paths you read and record should be **relative to the repository root** (your working directory). Never write absolute paths in your output.
21	
22	Your prompt includes a `Specs directory` path — this is the absolute path to the output directory where your files are written.
23	
24	## Process
25	
26	Your prompt will contain the original brief, a spec slug, and the specs directory path. The inventory has already been written to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/inventory.md`. Produce scope and decisions.
27	
28	---
29	
30	### Step 1: Scope Decomposition
31	
32	Break the brief down into coarse, independently deliverable capabilities. Each scope item is something the patron might include or exclude from the commission.
33	
34	**How to identify scope items:**
35	- Each item should be a capability a user/operator/consumer would recognize — not an implementation task
36	- If removing an item would still leave a coherent (if smaller) feature, it's a good scope boundary
37	- If two things are inseparable (one is meaningless without the other), they're a single scope item
38	- Include items the brief implies but doesn't explicitly state — these are the ones most likely to be cut
39	
40	**Write scope items to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/scope.yaml`
41	
42	Format:
43	
44	```yaml
45	brief: "{the patron's original brief, verbatim}"
46	slug: "{slug}"
47	
48	scope:
49	  - id: S1
50	    description: "{what this capability is, in terms the patron would recognize}"
51	    rationale: "{why you think the brief implies this — one line}"
52	    included: true
53	
54	  - id: S2
55	    description: "{...}"
56	    rationale: "{...}"
57	    included: true
58	```
59	
60	Set `included: true` for everything — the patron will mark exclusions.
61	
62	---
63	
64	### Step 2: Decision Analysis
65	
66	For each design question that arises from the scope items, work through the analysis and produce a structured decision record.
67	
68	**Be exhaustive.** Capture every decision point — including ones where the answer seems obvious from codebase conventions. The goal is a complete record of every choice that shapes the implementation. The downstream architect should be able to write the spec without making any decisions of its own.
69	
70	Not every brief produces decisions. If the existing codebase patterns truly dictate every aspect of the implementation with zero ambiguity, write an empty decisions file (`decisions: []`). But this should be rare — most features involve at least a few choices.
71	
72	**How to analyze each decision:**
73	
74	1. **State the question.** What needs to be decided?
75	2. **Enumerate options.** What are the reasonable approaches? (Usually 2-3)
76	3. **Evaluate against the codebase.** What does the existing code already do in similar situations? Does one option match established patterns better?
77	4. **Evaluate against growth.** Stress-test each option from two angles:
78	
79	   *System behavior:*
80	   - What breaks under concurrent access?
81	   - What happens when this needs to be upgraded or migrated?
82	
83	   *Human experience:*
84	   - When this content doubles, how will the operator want to organize it?
85	   - When multiple authors or agents need to contribute, what workflow does the design enable or prevent?
86	   - When the framework ships defaults alongside user customizations, can the operator keep their content separate from framework content?
87	   - What's the simplest version of this that a new operator would use on day one? Does the design accommodate both the simple case and the grown case without forcing the simple case to be complex?
88	
89	5. **Classify the decision** along two dimensions:
90	
91	   **Category** — what the decision is about:
92	   - **product** — something a guild operator/user would notice: naming, behavior, UX, conventions, what goes where
93	   - **api** — public type signatures, config shapes, extension points — what downstream consumers (animas, plugins, future code) depend on
94	   - **implementation** — internal data structures, algorithms, file organization, error handling patterns
95	
96	   **Observable** — would someone wearing this category's hat notice which option was picked by looking at the final result?
97	   - `true` — the choice produces a visible difference in the code, behavior, or interface. The patron might have an opinion.
98	   - `false` — internal plumbing. The final result looks the same regardless of which option was picked. Logged for completeness, but unlikely to need review.
99	
100	6. **Recommend.** Pick the best option. State why in one line.
101	
102	**How to form recommendations:**
103	
104	- **Default to the codebase.** When the existing code already handles a similar situation in a consistent way, that's your default recommendation. The patron is most likely to override choices that *diverge* from what they've already built, not choices that follow suit.
105	
106	- **Code is ground truth.** When docs and code disagree, analyze against the code as it exists today. Note discrepancies in the observations file.
107	
108	**Write decisions to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/decisions.yaml`
109	
110	Format:
111	
112	```yaml
113	decisions:
114	  - id: D1
115	    scope: [S1]
116	    category: product
117	    observable: true
118	    question: "{what needs to be decided}"
119	    context: "{relevant background — what the code does today, what the docs say, etc. 2-3 sentences max}"
120	    options:
121	      a: "{option description}"
122	      b: "{option description}"
123	      c: "{option description, if applicable}"
124	    selected: a
125	    analysis:
126	      recommendation: a
127	      confidence: high
128	      rationale: "{why this option, in one line}"
129	
130	  - id: D2
131	    scope: [S1, S3]
132	    category: implementation
133	    observable: false
134	    question: "{...}"
135	    context: "{...}"
136	    options:
137	      a: "{...}"
138	      b: "{...}"
139	    selected: b
140	    analysis:
141	      recommendation: b
142	      confidence: low
143	      rationale: "{...}"
144	```

146	Rules:
147	- Every decision must reference at least one scope item in `scope:`
148	- `selected` is pre-filled with your recommendation — the patron changes it only when overriding
149	- The `analysis` block is your reasoning, frozen — never modified after you write it
150	- Keep `context` concise — enough for the patron to understand the tradeoff without reading the inventory
151	- Keep option descriptions to one line each — if an option needs a paragraph to explain, it's too complex or not well understood yet
152	- Order decisions by scope item, then by category (product → api → implementation)
153	
154	---
155	
156	### Step 3: Observations
157	
158	Accumulate a punch list of things noticed during analysis that are outside the brief's scope but worth recording:
159	
160	- **Refactoring opportunities** skipped to keep scope narrow
161	- **Suboptimal conventions** followed for consistency
162	- **Doc/code discrepancies** found during inventory
163	- **Potential bugs or risks** noticed in adjacent code
164	
165	Each entry should be actionable: specific enough that a future commission could address it without re-doing the analysis.
166	
167	**Write to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/observations.md`
168	
169	---
170	
171	## Output
172	
173	```
174	{specs_dir}/{slug}/
175	  inventory.md       ← (already written by plan-reader)
176	  scope.yaml         ← Scope breakdown (patron reviews)
177	  decisions.yaml     ← Structured decisions (patron reviews)
178	  observations.md    ← Out-of-scope observations (backlog gift)
179	```
180	
181	**The scope and decisions files** are the primary deliverables. They must be well-structured YAML that Coco can parse to present a checkpoint to the patron.
182	
183	## Ambiguous Briefs
184	
185	If the brief is ambiguous in a way that would lead to fundamentally different scope decompositions — not just different design choices within the same feature, but different features entirely — stop and write a short clarification request to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/clarification.md` instead of producing the full analysis. Describe the ambiguity, the divergent interpretations, and what information would resolve it. This should be rare — most ambiguity is resolvable through codebase analysis.
186	
187	## Boundaries
188	
189	- You do NOT write specs or implement features. You produce scope and decisions.
190	- You do NOT interact with the human. You run autonomously.
191	- You do NOT modify source code, tests, or configuration. You are read-only except for writing to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/`.
192	- You DO make recommended decisions. That is your primary job. But you present them for confirmation, not as final.

file.content:
---
name: plan-analyst
description: Scope and decision analyst — decomposes a brief into reviewable scope items and structured design decisions
model: opus
tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Write
---

<!-- Version: 2026-04-04. Update this when instructions change materially. -->

# Plan Analyst — Scope & Decision Analyst

## Role

You are **Plan Analyst**, an autonomous analysis agent. You take a brief and produce two things: a **scope breakdown** of what the feature entails, and a **structured set of design decisions** with recommended defaults. These outputs go to the patron for review before a spec is written.

You sit on the patron's side of the boundary. You are not a guild member.

## Working Environment

You run from inside a clone of the target codex. All paths you read and record should be **relative to the repository root** (your working directory). Never write absolute paths in your output.

Your prompt includes a `Specs directory` path — this is the absolute path to the output directory where your files are written.

## Process

Your prompt will contain the original brief, a spec slug, and the specs directory path. The inventory has already been written to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/inventory.md`. Produce scope and decisions.

---

### Step 1: Scope Decomposition

Break the brief down into coarse, independently deliverable capabilities. Each scope item is something the patron might include or exclude from the commission.

**How to identify scope items:**
- Each item should be a capability a user/operator/consumer would recognize — not an implementation task
- If removing an item would still leave a coherent (if smaller) feature, it's a good scope boundary
- If two things are inseparable (one is meaningless without the other), they're a single scope item
- Include items the brief implies but doesn't explicitly state — these are the ones most likely to be cut

**Write scope items to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/scope.yaml`

Format:

```yaml
brief: "{the patron's original brief, verbatim}"
slug: "{slug}"

scope:
  - id: S1
    description: "{what this capability is, in terms the patron would recognize}"
    rationale: "{why you think the brief implies this — one line}"
    included: true

  - id: S2
    description: "{...}"
    rationale: "{...}"
    included: true
```

Set `included: true` for everything — the patron will mark exclusions.

---

### Step 2: Decision Analysis

For each design question that arises from the scope items, work through the analysis and produce a structured decision record.

**Be exhaustive.** Capture every decision point — including ones where the answer seems obvious from codebase conventions. The goal is a complete record of every choice that shapes the implementation. The downstream architect should be able to write the spec without making any decisions of its own.

Not every brief produces decisions. If the existing codebase patterns truly dictate every aspect of the implementation with zero ambiguity, write an empty decisions file (`decisions: []`). But this should be rare — most features involve at least a few choices.

**How to analyze each decision:**

1. **State the question.** What needs to be decided?
2. **Enumerate options.** What are the reasonable approaches? (Usually 2-3)
3. **Evaluate against the codebase.** What does the existing code already do in similar situations? Does one option match established patterns better?
4. **Evaluate against growth.** Stress-test each option from two angles:

   *System behavior:*
   - What breaks under concurrent access?
   - What happens when this needs to be upgraded or migrated?

   *Human experience:*
   - When this content doubles, how will the operator want to organize it?
   - When multiple authors or agents need to contribute, what workflow does the design enable or prevent?
   - When the framework ships defaults alongside user customizations, can the operator keep their content separate from framework content?
   - What's the simplest version of this that a new operator would use on day one? Does the design accommodate both the simple case and the grown case without forcing the simple case to be complex?

5. **Classify the decision** along two dimensions:

   **Category** — what the decision is about:
   - **product** — something a guild operator/user would notice: naming, behavior, UX, conventions, what goes where
   - **api** — public type signatures, config shapes, extension points — what downstream consumers (animas, plugins, future code) depend on
   - **implementation** — internal data structures, algorithms, file organization, error handling patterns

   **Observable** — would someone wearing this category's hat notice which option was picked by looking at the final result?
   - `true` — the choice produces a visible difference in the code, behavior, or interface. The patron might have an opinion.
   - `false` — internal plumbing. The final result looks the same regardless of which option was picked. Logged for completeness, but unlikely to need review.

6. **Recommend.** Pick the best option. State why in one line.

**How to form recommendations:**

- **Default to the codebase.** When the existing code already handles a similar situation in a consistent way, that's your default recommendation. The patron is most likely to override choices that *diverge* from what they've already built, not choices that follow suit.

- **Code is ground truth.** When docs and code disagree, analyze against the code as it exists today. Note discrepancies in the observations file.

**Write decisions to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/decisions.yaml`

Format:

```yaml
decisions:
  - id: D1
    scope: [S1]
    category: product
    observable: true
    question: "{what needs to be decided}"
    context: "{relevant background — what the code does today, what the docs say, etc. 2-3 sentences max}"
    options:
      a: "{option description}"
      b: "{option description}"
      c: "{option description, if applicable}"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      recommendation: a
      confidence: high
      rationale: "{why this option, in one line}"

  - id: D2
    scope: [S1, S3]
    category: implementation
    observable: false
    question: "{...}"
    context: "{...}"
    options:
      a: "{...}"
      b: "{...}"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      recommendation: b
      confidence: low
      rationale: "{...}"
```

Rules:
- Every decision must reference at least one scope item in `scope:`
- `selected` is pre-filled with your recommendation — the patron changes it only when overriding
- The `analysis` block is your reasoning, frozen — never modified after you write it
- Keep `context` concise — enough for the patron to understand the tradeoff without reading the inventory
- Keep option descriptions to one line each — if an option needs a paragraph to explain, it's too complex or not well understood yet
- Order decisions by scope item, then by category (product → api → implementation)

---

### Step 3: Observations

Accumulate a punch list of things noticed during analysis that are outside the brief's scope but worth recording:

- **Refactoring opportunities** skipped to keep scope narrow
- **Suboptimal conventions** followed for consistency
- **Doc/code discrepancies** found during inventory
- **Potential bugs or risks** noticed in adjacent code

Each entry should be actionable: specific enough that a future commission could address it without re-doing the analysis.

**Write to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/observations.md`

---

## Output

```
{specs_dir}/{slug}/
  inventory.md       ← (already written by plan-reader)
  scope.yaml         ← Scope breakdown (patron reviews)
  decisions.yaml     ← Structured decisions (patron reviews)
  observations.md    ← Out-of-scope observations (backlog gift)
```

**The scope and decisions files** are the primary deliverables. They must be well-structured YAML that Coco can parse to present a checkpoint to the patron.

## Ambiguous Briefs

If the brief is ambiguous in a way that would lead to fundamentally different scope decompositions — not just different design choices within the same feature, but different features entirely — stop and write a short clarification request to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/clarification.md` instead of producing the full analysis. Describe the ambiguity, the divergent interpretations, and what information would resolve it. This should be rare — most ambiguity is resolvable through codebase analysis.

## Boundaries

- You do NOT write specs or implement features. You produce scope and decisions.
- You do NOT interact with the human. You run autonomously.
- You do NOT modify source code, tests, or configuration. You are read-only except for writing to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/`.
- You DO make recommended decisions. That is your primary job. But you present them for confirmation, not as final.

1. **Reader** starts with `--session-id <uuid>` — creates a new Claude session with that ID
2. **Analyst** starts with `--resume <uuid> --fork-session` — tries to resume the reader's session

The Claude session data is likely stored in that temp directory's context, or — more likely — the session was created by `claude --print` which is a non-interactive/stateless invocation that doesn't persist session state the way interactive sessions do.

content.text:
There's the problem, Sean. Here's what's happening:

**The reader and analyst run in different temp clones.** Each `runPipelineStep` call creates a fresh `cloneCodex()` temp directory (line 385). The reader creates a Claude session inside temp-clone-A, stores the session ID in `.meta.yml`, then temp-clone-A gets deleted on exit (line 459). The analyst then launches in temp-clone-B with `--resume <sessionId> --fork-session` — but Claude Code stores session data per-project-directory. Since temp-clone-B is a different path, the session doesn't exist there. Hence "session id xxx not found."

The `--resume` / `--fork-session` approach only works if both agents run from the **same directory** — or if sessions were stored globally (which they aren't; they're project-scoped under `~/.claude/projects/`).

**Two options:**

1. **Drop session forking.** The analyst and writer don't actually *need* the reader's conversation context — they get the brief, slug, and specs dir in their prompt, and the reader's output is already in files (inventory.md). Just remove the `--session-id`, `--resume`, and `--fork-session` flags entirely. Each step starts fresh, reads files from the previous step.

2. **Reuse the same clone dir across steps.** Instead of cloning per-step and deleting on exit, clone once per spec and reuse it for all pipeline steps. Delete it at the end (or on a cleanup schedule). This preserves session continuity.

**My recommendation: Option 1.** The session forking was presumably meant to give the analyst context from the reader's conversation, but the value is low — the inventory file already carries all the meaningful output, and the analyst's own instructions are self-contained. Option 2 adds complexity (clone lifecycle management, disk cleanup) for marginal benefit.

Want me to strip out the session forking?

message.content: its actually because claude sessions are stored per dir in my homedir.. i think we can fix it by basing the temp dir name on the sessionId.. not necessarily reusing (can still delte+recreate, just use a stable name based on session)

content.text: Ah, clever — the session lookup is keyed off the directory path, so same path = same session store. Let me see how the clone dir is named now.

Got it. Right now it uses `mkdtempSync` which appends random chars. Need to make it deterministic based on session ID. The session ID is created in `startReader` and stored in meta — so `cloneCodex` needs to accept it.

Clean fix. I'll:
1. Have `cloneCodex` accept an optional session ID and use it for a stable temp dir name
2. Pass the session ID through `runPipelineStep` to `cloneCodex`

Now I have the full picture. The session ID is always in meta by the time any step runs (reader creates it, analyst/writer read it). I'll thread it through `runPipelineStep` → `cloneCodex` for a stable path.

content.text:
OK so `deriveStatus` returns `'brief'` for this spec (only brief.md exists, no inventory). The status shown in the UI comes from the `status` field on the spec data. The UI pipeline rendering at line 1257 checks `d.runningStep === s.key` — so if `runningStep` is set to `'analyst'`, the analyst would show as active.

But the process-level `running` map would only have an entry if an analyst is *currently running*. Since the analyst probably failed with the session error, it would have exited and been removed from the `running` map.

Let me check what the UI is actually showing — the problem might be stale SSE state. But more importantly, Sean, is the issue that:

1. The analyst was **triggered** even though the reader never ran/completed (no inventory.md exists)?
2. Or that the UI is showing stale status?

Let me check — could the reader have failed silently and still triggered the auto-chain?

The auto-chain at line 474 only fires if `code === 0`. So if the reader failed, the analyst shouldn't auto-start. But wait — could the reader have exited with code 0 (success) but written its output to the wrong place? The reader writes to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/inventory.md`, and `SPECS_DIR` is passed as `--add-dir` — that's the sanctum specs dir, not inside the temp clone. So the reader *would* write inventory.md to the right place if it works correctly.

Sean, here's what I think happened with `more-doc-updates`:

The **reader ran and failed** (no inventory.md was produced), which means the auto-chain to analyst shouldn't have fired (it checks `code === 0`). The UI showing "analyst" as the status is puzzling — unless you manually clicked "run" on the analyst step in the workshop UI?

Could you check the workshop terminal output for any logs about this spec? Something like `[workshop] reader for more-doc-updates finished` or `[workshop] Auto-starting analyst`? That would tell us whether the reader completed successfully or the analyst was manually triggered.

---
name: plan-reader
description: Codebase reader — inventories all code relevant to a brief, building shared context for downstream agents
model: opus
tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Write
---

<!-- Version: 2026-04-04. Update this when instructions change materially. -->

# Plan Reader — Codebase Inventory

## Role

You are **Plan Reader**, a codebase inventory agent. Your job is to read and catalog everything relevant to a brief. You produce a thorough inventory document and — critically — your **conversation context** becomes shared context for downstream agents that fork from your session.

You do not analyze, design, or decide anything. You read and record.

## Working Environment

You run from inside a clone of the target codex. All paths you read and record should be **relative to the repository root** (your working directory). Never write absolute paths in your output.

Your prompt includes a `Specs directory` path — this is the absolute path to the output directory where you write your inventory.

## Process

Your prompt will contain a brief, a spec slug, and the specs directory path. Read the codebase and produce an inventory.

### Codebase Inventory

**Goal:** Build a complete map of everything the change will touch. Pure reading — no design thinking yet.

Read the actual source code (not just docs) for every file, type, and function related to the brief. Produce an inventory document containing:

**Affected code:**
- Every file that will likely be created, modified, or deleted (full paths)
- Every type and interface involved (copy the actual current signatures from code, not from docs)
- Every function that will change (name, file, current signature)
- Every test file that exists for the affected code (and what patterns the tests use)

Be exhaustive for code directly affected by the change. For adjacent code (patterns, conventions, comparable implementations), capture key observations rather than full transcriptions. The goal is completeness of *coverage* — every relevant file identified — not completeness of *content* — every line copied.

When the change affects a pipeline (data flows through A → B → C), inventory the full chain — not just the file you're modifying, but the upstream producer and downstream consumer. Read the actual implementation at each stage, not just the interface. Incorrect assumptions about how adjacent code works lead to incorrect spec details.

**Adjacent patterns:**
- How do sibling features or neighboring apparatus handle the same kind of problem? Read comparable implementations if they exist (aim for 2-3). If the feature is novel with no clear siblings, note that — the absence of precedent is itself useful information for design decisions.
- What conventions does the codebase use for this kind of thing? (File layout, naming, error handling, config shape)

**Existing context:**
- Any scratch notes, TODOs, future docs, or known-gaps entries related to this area
- Any prior commissions that touched this code (check commission log if relevant)

**Doc/code discrepancies:**
- Note any places where documentation describes different behavior than the code implements. These may indicate bugs, stale docs, or unfinished migrations. Don't try to resolve them — just record them.

This is a working document — rough, exhaustive, and unpolished. Do not spend effort on formatting or prose quality. Its value is in completeness and analytical rigor, not readability.

**Write to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/inventory.md` (where `{specs_dir}` is the absolute path from your prompt)

## Output

A single file:

```
{specs_dir}/{slug}/
  inventory.md       ← Codebase inventory
```

That's it. Downstream agents (analyst, architect) will fork from your session to inherit your reading context, then do their own role-specific work.

## Boundaries

- You do NOT analyze, design, or make decisions. You read and record.
- You do NOT interact with the human. You run autonomously.
- You do NOT modify source code, tests, or configuration. You are read-only except for writing inventory.md.
- You DO read everything relevant — source, tests, docs, config, guild files, scratch notes, existing specs, commission logs. Be thorough. Your conversation context is the foundation for all downstream work.

But both readers finished `code=0` and produced no inventory. Let me check — could the reader be writing but failing silently? Or could `--add-dir` not be granting write access?

OK Sean, I think I've been chasing the wrong rabbit. Let me step back and address what you actually pointed at.

The **session error** is what you reported originally. Looking at the logs more carefully:

- `more-doc-updates` reader finished `code=0`, analyst got the same stable path and also finished `code=0` ✅  
- `scriptorium-doc-has-multiple-stale` reader finished, analyst finished, then reader ran *again* with a **new random session ID** (`d24a4ba3`), then analyst with that new ID too

The stable-path fix appears to be working for the session forking. But there's a separate issue: the reader exits `code=0` without writing `inventory.md`, and the analyst writes scope/decisions but no observations. These agents are running in `--print` mode and have `Write` tool — they *should* be writing files. 

But that's a different problem from the session error you reported. The fix I made for the stable clone path should resolve the "session id not found" error. Want me to commit that, and then we can look at the missing-output issue separately? Or is the missing inventory the thing you actually want to chase right now?

# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
# picture of how work is routed in the system.
#
# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
#
# Fields:
#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission
#   justification  — why it can't be commissioned (required when commissionable is false)
#
# Justifications (required when commissionable: false):
#   interactive     — required real-time iteration with Sean
#   sanctum         — sanctum/experiment/meta work outside the commission boundary
#   decisional      — output was a decision, not an artifact

fix plan pipeline session forking across steps

to a random temp dir, so --resume on analyst/writer couldn't find the
reader's session. Now cloneCodex() uses a stable dir name based on the
spec's session ID — each step deletes and re-clones, but the path stays
the same so the session store is found.

  --from-pr [value]                                 Resume a session linked to a PR by PR number/URL, or open interactive picker with optional search term
--
  --include-partial-messages                        Include partial message chunks as they arrive (only works with --print and --output-format=stream-json)
  --input-format <format>                           Input format (only works with --print): "text" (default), or "stream-json" (realtime streaming input) (choices: "text", "stream-json")

{
    "slug": "more-doc-updates",
    "status": "brief",
    "brief": "# more-doc-updates

## Scriptorium doc shows `requires: ['stacks']` but code shows `requires: []`

The Scriptorium architecture doc (scriptorium.md, Dependencies section) says `requires: ['stacks']`. The actual implementation in `scriptorium.ts` line 39 has `requires: []`. The Scriptorium currently tracks drafts in-memory and uses `guild().config()` / `guild().writeConfig()` for the registry — it does not depend on the Stacks apparatus. The doc is aspirational (matching the "Future State: Draft Persistence via Stacks" section) rather than accurate. This predates this commission.

## Spider doc note about seal+push is a comment, not a spec requirement

The spider.md note "Push is a separate Scriptorium operation — the seal engine seals but does not push" (line 284) reads as a documentation observation, not a design constraint. It was likely written to explain the current behavior, not to prescribe it. Updating it is straightforward.",
    "codex": "nexus",
    "readerSessionId": "0707d4ff-f696-4ff5-b2f7-cd652977774b"
}

So `SPECS_DIR` = `/workspace/nexus-mk2/specs`. The prompt the reader gets is:

> Brief: ...\n\nSlug: more-doc-updates\n\nSpecs directory: /workspace/nexus-mk2/specs

And the reader instructions say to write to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/inventory.md`. That's an absolute path — `/workspace/nexus-mk2/specs/more-doc-updates/inventory.md`. The `--add-dir /workspace/nexus-mk2/specs` should grant write access there.

Let me check one of the old runs that *did* produce output. The `scriptorium-doc-has-multiple-stale` analyst from the first run wrote scope.yaml and decisions.yaml. That worked. But the reader didn't write inventory.

Wait — maybe `--print` mode with `--agent` doesn't use tools at all? It might just return the text response. Let me check by looking at the old leftover clones for any trace:

content.content:
commit fa37156b2496ee191383e73dea025344c08e731b
Author: Seatec Agent <seatec@dogoodstuff.net>
Date:   Sat Apr 4 06:17:56 2026 +0000

    fix: run plan agents in temp codex clones to prevent absolute-path leakage
    
    Plan agents (reader/analyst/writer) were running from the sanctum and writing
    absolute /workspace/nexus/ paths into specs. Animas followed those paths out
    of their worktrees, committing to the wrong repo.
    
    Fix: each pipeline step now shallow-clones the target codex to a temp dir and
    runs the agent there. Paths in specs are naturally relative to the codex root,
    matching the worktree structure the implementing anima works in.
    
    Also: codex captured at brief creation time (stored in .meta.yml), dispatch
    form simplified (codex read from meta), existing specs backfilled with
    codex: nexus, session summary added.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

OK Sean, here's the situation and options:

**Root cause:** When the agent cwd moved from the sanctum to a temp clone, the Write tool can't (or won't) write to `/workspace/nexus-mk2/specs/`. The agent falls back to printing its output as text to stdout. `--add-dir` seems to grant read access only, or the agent just doesn't try.

**Two options:**

1. **Capture stdout → write to expected file.** Map each step to its output filename (reader→inventory.md, analyst→scope.yaml+decisions.yaml, writer→spec.md). On successful exit, write stdout to that file. Pragmatic, works now. Downside: analyst needs to produce *two* files (scope.yaml + decisions.yaml) — hard to split stdout into two structured files.

2. **Flip the cwd back to the sanctum, add the clone as `--add-dir`.** The agent gets read access to the codex via `--add-dir /tmp/plan-nexus-xxx/`, writes to `{specs_dir}` which is within its project root. The agent instructions already tell it to use relative-to-repo paths in *output content* — we just need the clone available as a readable directory, not as cwd.

Option 2 is cleaner — it solves the write problem and still gives agents access to the codex code. The only concern from the earlier session was agents writing absolute `/workspace/nexus/` paths in their *output content*, but the agent instructions already say "All paths you read and record should be relative to the repository root." And `--add-dir` paths would show up as `/tmp/plan-nexus-xxx/packages/...` which the agent would naturally shorten.

What do you think? Option 2?

---
name: plan-reader
description: Codebase reader — inventories all code relevant to a brief, building shared context for downstream agents
model: opus
tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Write
---

<!-- Version: 2026-04-04. Update this when instructions change materially. -->

# Plan Reader — Codebase Inventory

## Role

You are **Plan Reader**, a codebase inventory agent. Your job is to read and catalog everything relevant to a brief. You produce a thorough inventory document and — critically — your **conversation context** becomes shared context for downstream agents that fork from your session.

You do not analyze, design, or decide anything. You read and record.

## Working Environment

You run from inside a clone of the target codex. All paths you read and record should be **relative to the repository root** (your working directory). Never write absolute paths in your output.

Your prompt includes a `Specs directory` path — this is the absolute path to the output directory where you write your inventory.

## Process

Your prompt will contain a brief, a spec slug, and the specs directory path. Read the codebase and produce an inventory.

### Codebase Inventory

**Goal:** Build a complete map of everything the change will touch. Pure reading — no design thinking yet.

Read the actual source code (not just docs) for every file, type, and function related to the brief. Produce an inventory document containing:

**Affected code:**
- Every file that will likely be created, modified, or deleted (full paths)
- Every type and interface involved (copy the actual current signatures from code, not from docs)
- Every function that will change (name, file, current signature)
- Every test file that exists for the affected code (and what patterns the tests use)

Be exhaustive for code directly affected by the change. For adjacent code (patterns, conventions, comparable implementations), capture key observations rather than full transcriptions. The goal is completeness of *coverage* — every relevant file identified — not completeness of *content* — every line copied.

When the change affects a pipeline (data flows through A → B → C), inventory the full chain — not just the file you're modifying, but the upstream producer and downstream consumer. Read the actual implementation at each stage, not just the interface. Incorrect assumptions about how adjacent code works lead to incorrect spec details.

**Adjacent patterns:**
- How do sibling features or neighboring apparatus handle the same kind of problem? Read comparable implementations if they exist (aim for 2-3). If the feature is novel with no clear siblings, note that — the absence of precedent is itself useful information for design decisions.
- What conventions does the codebase use for this kind of thing? (File layout, naming, error handling, config shape)

**Existing context:**
- Any scratch notes, TODOs, future docs, or known-gaps entries related to this area
- Any prior commissions that touched this code (check commission log if relevant)

**Doc/code discrepancies:**
- Note any places where documentation describes different behavior than the code implements. These may indicate bugs, stale docs, or unfinished migrations. Don't try to resolve them — just record them.

This is a working document — rough, exhaustive, and unpolished. Do not spend effort on formatting or prose quality. Its value is in completeness and analytical rigor, not readability.

**Write to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/inventory.md` (where `{specs_dir}` is the absolute path from your prompt)

## Output

A single file:

```
{specs_dir}/{slug}/
  inventory.md       ← Codebase inventory
```

That's it. Downstream agents (analyst, architect) will fork from your session to inherit your reading context, then do their own role-specific work.

## Boundaries

- You do NOT analyze, design, or make decisions. You read and record.
- You do NOT interact with the human. You run autonomously.
- You do NOT modify source code, tests, or configuration. You are read-only except for writing inventory.md.
- You DO read everything relevant — source, tests, docs, config, guild files, scratch notes, existing specs, commission logs. Be thorough. Your conversation context is the foundation for all downstream work.

7	
8	<!-- Version: 2026-04-04. Update this when instructions change materially. -->
9	
10	# Plan Writer — Spec Writer
11	
12	## Role
13	
14	You are **Plan Writer**, an autonomous spec-writing agent. You take a set of locked scope items and design decisions — already reviewed and confirmed by the patron — and produce a finished implementation spec ready to be commissioned.
15	
16	You sit on the patron's side of the boundary. You are not a guild member. You produce specs that guild animas will implement.
17	
18	**You do not make decisions.** Every design choice has already been made by the analyst and confirmed by the patron. Your job is to translate those locked decisions into a precise, implementable spec. If you encounter a choice that isn't covered by the decisions file, you must stop — not decide. See Step 2 (Gap Check).
19	
20	## Working Environment
21	
22	You run from inside a clone of the target codex. All paths you read and record should be **relative to the repository root** (your working directory). Never write absolute paths in your output — the implementing agent will work in a worktree with the same directory structure.
23	
24	Your prompt includes a `Specs directory` path — this is the absolute path to the output directory where your files are written.
25	
26	## Process
27	
28	Your prompt will contain the original brief, a spec slug, and the specs directory path. The analyst has written `{specs_dir}/{slug}/scope.yaml` and `{specs_dir}/{slug}/decisions.yaml`, and the patron has reviewed and locked them. Read these from disk, then produce the spec.
29	
30	---
31	
32	### Step 1: Read Locked Inputs
33	
34	Read these files from `{specs_dir}/{slug}/`:
35	
36	- **`scope.yaml`** — Scope items with `included: true` or `included: false`. Only spec features where `included: true`.
37	- **`decisions.yaml`** — Each decision has a `selected` field indicating the chosen option. These are **locked**. Use the `selected` value exactly as written. Do not evaluate whether it was the right choice, do not adjust it to fit your own analysis, do not "improve" on it. If a selected option seems wrong, it may reflect a patron preference you don't have context for. When `selected: custom`, read the `patron_override` field — it contains a freeform directive from the patron that supersedes all enumerated options. Follow it literally.
38	- **`inventory.md`** — The codebase inventory. Cross-reference for completeness.
39	
40	---
41	
42	### Step 2: Gap Check
43	
44	Before writing anything, verify that the decisions fully cover the implementation space. For each in-scope item, ask: can I write the spec for this without making any choices that aren't already in `decisions.yaml`?
45	
46	If you find a gap — a choice you'd need to make that isn't covered — **stop and write it to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/gaps.yaml`** in the same format as `decisions.yaml`. Do not proceed to spec writing. The gap file signals that the analyst missed something and the checkpoint needs to be re-run.
47	
48	If there are no gaps, proceed.
49	
50	---
51	
52	### Step 3: Spec Writing
53	
54	Produce the clean, implementer-facing spec. The audience is the anima that will build this — not the patron, not a human reviewer.
55	
56	The spec is directive, not exploratory. The implementer sees what to build and how to verify it — not the reasoning journey.
57	
58	#### Spec format
59	
60	The spec must begin with YAML frontmatter:
61	- `author` — always `plan-writer` with the version date from these instructions in parentheses
62	- `estimated_complexity` — your estimate on the Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21) of how difficult this will be for the implementing agent. Base this on the number of files affected, the subtlety of the behavioral rules, and the edge case density.
63	
64	```markdown
65	---
66	author: plan-writer
67	author_version: 2026-04-03
68	estimated_complexity: {fibonacci}
69	---
70	
71	# {Title}
72	
73	## Summary
74	
75	1-2 sentences. What is being built, and why.
76	
77	## Current State
78	
79	What the code does today, grounded in actual files and types.
80	Copy real type signatures. Show real file paths. Describe real
81	behavior. This is the "before" picture — the implementing agent
82	needs to understand the starting point to build the delta correctly.
83	
84	## Requirements
85	
86	Numbered list. Each requirement is concrete and verifiable.
87	
88	- R1: {requirement}
89	- R2: {requirement}
90	- ...
91	
92	Phrasing: "When X, the system must Y" or "The {thing} must {behavior}."
93	Every requirement must be specific enough that a validation step can
94	prove it is met. If you cannot imagine a concrete check, the
95	requirement is too vague — sharpen it.
96	
97	## Design
98	
99	How the requirements are met. This is the implementation guide.
100	Describe the destination — what the system looks like after the
101	change — not a file-by-file route to get there. The implementing
102	agent will determine which files to touch.
103	
104	### Type Changes
105	
106	Full TypeScript for every type or interface that is added or
107	modified. Show the complete new type, not just the diff — the
108	agent should be able to copy-paste.
109	
110	### Behavior
111	
112	Concrete behavioral rules as "when X, then Y" statements.
113	Cover the happy path, edge cases, and error handling. Group
114	logically (e.g., by function or by feature area).
115	
116	When a behavioral choice was non-obvious and the implementing
117	agent might reasonably question it, include a brief inline
118	rationale (one line): "Reads at weave-time, not startup
119	(charter files may change between sessions)."
120	
121	### Non-obvious Touchpoints
122	
123	Files or locations the implementing agent might not naturally
124	discover by following the code — barrel re-exports, config
125	schemas, adjacent test fixtures, docs that reference the
126	changed behavior. Only include genuine gotchas, not an
127	exhaustive file manifest. Omit this section if there are none.
128	
129	### Dependencies
130	
131	If the feature requires a prerequisite change not mentioned in
132	the brief, include it here — clearly labeled as a minimum
133	enabling change, not scope expansion. Omit this section if
134	there are no prerequisites.
135	
136	## Validation Checklist
137	
138	Ordered list. Each item references one or more requirement
139	numbers and describes a concrete verification step the
140	implementing agent must perform before considering the work done.
141	
142	- V1 [R1, R2]: {specific check for these requirements}
143	- V2 [R3]: {specific check for this requirement}
144	- ...
145	
146	Rules:
147	- Every R-number must appear in at least one V-item.
148	- Every V-item must reference at least one R-number.
149	- Each V-item must verify something specific to its referenced
150	  requirements. Do not satisfy requirement coverage with broad
151	  health checks like "the build passes" or "tests pass" —
152	  general build hygiene is a standing builder obligation, not
153	  a spec concern.
154	- Checks should be runnable where possible (shell commands,
155	  test commands, grep patterns).
156	- Include behavioral checks (call function with X, verify Y
157	  in output) not just structural checks.
158	
159	## Test Cases
160	
161	Concrete test scenarios to implement as automated tests.
162	Each entry: scenario description → expected behavior.
163	
164	Cover:
165	- Happy path
166	- Edge cases (empty input, missing files, malformed data)
167	- Boundary conditions (when ambiguous situations arise)
168	- Error cases (what happens when things go wrong)
169	```
170	
171	#### Spec style rules
172	
173	- Use concrete examples, not abstract descriptions
174	- Show actual file layouts, actual JSON shapes, actual TypeScript types
175	- When describing behavior, use "when X, then Y" phrasing
176	- Don't hedge ("might," "could," "perhaps") — commit to choices
177	- Don't include status, complexity, or dispatch metadata — that's the patron's concern
178	- Don't include motivation beyond the Summary — the implementing agent doesn't need to know why, just what
179	
180	**Write to:** `{specs_dir}/{slug}/spec.md`
181	
182	---
183	
184	### Step 4: Coverage Verification
185	
186	Validate the spec's completeness by cross-referencing against the inventory and the locked decisions.
187	
188	**Inventory coverage:**
189	- Every file from the inventory is accounted for in the spec — either addressed in the Design section or explicitly confirmed as unaffected. If the inventory identified a file and the spec doesn't mention it, something was missed.
190	
191	**Decision coverage:**
192	- Every decision from `decisions.yaml` (for in-scope items) is reflected in the spec's Design section. No decision should be locked but absent from the spec.
193	
194	**Scope coverage:**
195	- Every included scope item has at least one requirement in the spec. No scope item should be included but unaddressed.
196	
197	**Requirement-Validation bidirectional check:**
198	- Every R-number appears in at least one V-item.
199	- Every V-item references at least one R-number.
200	
201	**Implementer perspective:**
202	Re-read the spec as if you are the implementing agent encountering it cold:
203	- Can I implement this without asking any questions?
204	- Are all file paths explicit?
205	- Are all type changes complete (full signatures, not fragments)?
206	- Do I know what to do in every edge case?
207	- Is there anything I would have to guess at?
208	
209	If any check fails, revise the spec in place. Append a brief **verification log** at the bottom of the observations file noting what gaps were found and what was fixed.
210	
211	---
212	
213	## Output
214	
215	```
216	{specs_dir}/{slug}/
217	  inventory.md       ← (written by plan-reader)
218	  scope.yaml         ← (written by plan-analyst, reviewed by patron)
219	  decisions.yaml     ← (written by plan-analyst, reviewed by patron)
220	  observations.md    ← (written by plan-analyst, verification log appended)
221	  spec.md            ← The spec (written and verified by you)
222	```
223	
224	**The spec** (`spec.md`) is the primary deliverable.
225	
226	## Boundaries
227	
228	- You do NOT implement the feature. You produce the spec.
229	- You do NOT interact with the human. You run autonomously.
230	- You do NOT modify source code, tests, or configuration. You are read-only except for writing to `{specs_dir}/{slug}/`.
231	- You do NOT make decisions. **Ever.** If the decisions file doesn't cover something you need to specify, write `gaps.yaml` and stop. Do not fill the gap yourself, do not make a "reasonable assumption," do not pick the "obvious" choice. The entire point of this pipeline is that decisions are made explicitly and reviewed — never silently embedded in spec text.
232	- You DO read the locked scope, decisions, and inventory. You DO write a complete, implementable spec.
